turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: We'll hear argument first this morning number oh one four sixty-three, United States versus Fior D'Italia. Ms. O'Connor.

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court. This case is about the authority of the Commissioner of Internal Revenue to assess FICA taxes that Congress has imposed on employers. It also involves the well established principle of tax litigation. It involves two key sections of the Internal Revenue Code and how they relate to each other, and it involves the evidentiary value of assessments in tax litigation. The first of the key sections is section sixty-two oh one. It appears at page sixty-two A of the appendix to our petition. It authorizes and requires the Secretary of the Treasury to make inquiries, determinations, and assessments of all taxes imposed by the Internal Revenue Code. The second key section is thirty-one eleven. It appears at page fifty-five A of the appendix to our petition. This section imposes a tax on employers. The tax is measured by the wages they pay or are deemed to have paid their employees.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: Wh- which section is this, Ms. O'Connor? I...

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: Section thirty-one eleven at pa- page fifty-five A of our appendix to the petition...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: This is the tax that imposes this is the provision that imposes a tax on the wages employers pay or are deemed to have paid their employees. The question this case presents is how the commissioner carries out his obligation under sixty-two oh one to assess the tax that is imposed by section thirty-one eleven. [Inaudible]

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: The tax in... Ms. O'Connor...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: let me tell you what troubles me a little about the Government's position in the case, and I hope you will address it.

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: Exactly, Your Honor.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: Well, would that error ever be anything more than a disparity between what the employee reported and what the employer reported?

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: Would what, Your Honor?

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: How am I mean the the error that that section con- I'm wondering what the error is that that section contemplates, and I can I can see it's application, let's say, if the employer simply added up the employee's reports wrong and got the wrong figure.

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: It could also apply just an- and sixty-two oh five is not specific to restaurants or food or beverage establishments or or tips at all, because another common error that employers can sometimes make is mis-characterizing a worker as an independent contractor, for example, and upon determining later that the person is actually an employee and they should be withholding...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: FICA...

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: the question in another way.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: In thi- -- how come...

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: as I understand it I mean, this is th- the I didn't didn't mean to side-track you from Justice O'Connor's question, but I'll I'll raise the thing that bothers me.

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: That's right, Your Honor.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: and I'll assume for the sake of the question that a given employer has done that.

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: Mhm.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: I also assume that if you are correct, that employer has this possibility hanging over it that that that the IRS is is going to make this kind of an assessment.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: That's that...

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: M- my question is, is there any way that the employer can anticipate this kind of an assessment in order at least to to avoid the interest running under under this section?

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: Absolutely.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: And they were all correct, I take it.

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: Right, those forms arrived absolutely correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: January. You start setting aside for it.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: He may set up a res- the employer may set up a reserve,

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: Mhm.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: but as I understand it the employer is not obligated, in in effect, to to withhold upon himself, is not obligated to pay the tax on it until the assessment comes, because under under the IRS's instructions the employer pays the tax on the amounts reported to the employer even if he knows they're wrong.

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: That's exactly right, and that's another indication of the fact that Congress realized that this was going to be a difficult provision for employers to enact.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: Well, what would...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: he do -- What...

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: would he do to anticipate it? Would he would he say I'm I'm paying you another ten thousand dollars, I don't happen to owe you that in tax now, I've paid what the law requires me to pay, and you haven't assessed anything else.

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: Absolutely not, Your Honor.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: All right, then how does the taxpayer take advantage of the provision that allows him to make a correction and avoid the interest in this case?

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: Well, one of the things that is somewhat troublesome about some of the briefs in front of you from the other side is that the other side is looking at this as though it were a penalty.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: something...

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: No, I will assume it's it's no penalty.

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: Uh-huh.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: I just want to know how it works in this situation.

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: based on what he has to report to you.

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: You haven't assessed anything yet.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: take advantage of it?

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: let me point out a couple of things from your question, Justice Souter.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Wh- where is twenty-one thirty-one...

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: is in our joint appendix Your -- oh, no, no, it's in the petition.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: It's at page fif-

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: help if you answered specifically whether interest does run on the period between the employer paying what the employee's reports call for and the assessment at a higher amount.

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: Thank you, Justice Ginsburg.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: twenty-one Q in particular.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: Then then how does this section have an application here? You say, well, this mitigates it,

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: No, I'm sorry. If if...

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: the employer can do is pay immediately at the moment of your assessment and therefore no interest...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: will run...

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: No, I did not mean to suggest that sixty-two oh five applied to this assessment, and in fact it doesn't.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: opportunity.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: wouldn't apply in these circumstances.

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: It doesn't need to, because under thirty-one twenty-one Q it's a very interesting provision.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: penalty.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: that point there is, and if you would come back to Justice O'Connor's question, which was, how could the employer ever know that there was a mistake in the assessment? And and you said, well, as soon as he knows it, he can come forward with the additional amount and there won't be any penalty, but how does he know it?

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: That's the problem.

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: reporting on a monthly basis, and in this case there was clearly you know, we have a hundred and twenty thousand dollars of unreported credit card tips in each of the two years involved here.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: The restaurant has clear notice...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Your a- your assessment assumed a certain unreported amount of of cash tips as well.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Yes, it did, Your Honor.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: how is the reporter how is the employer going to know whether that's erroneous or not?

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: The same method that he would use to make any other determination.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: On that s- wait...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: But the burden is on him.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: Justice Scalia, the burden is on the taxpayer, and in this case the taxpayer conceded the ent- the entire amount of the adjustment...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Page thirty-five of the joint appendix --

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: is on the taxpayer to give information as to which the taxpayer is not in the best p- p- pos- position to know.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: That's true, Justice Kennedy. The -- uh-huh.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: how is the employer supposed to know, and you say, well, if he doesn't know he can amend later.

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: Actually, n- not all of that is true is Justice Kennedy.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: say, does...

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: how does he do that, s- s- s- sue the em-

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: employee or something?

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: Oh, if in i- in yes, if if there were a dispute between the two over any matter the employer certainly could.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Well you're...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: You're talking about a tax court or...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: district court or...

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: Just in just as a general proposition.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: general proposition I don't think the employer can s- s- s- subpoena the employee.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Don't you.

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: That's what I meant, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: If if there were some other legal proceeding.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: he ought to fire the employee and then be subjected to a wrongful discharge suit and then subpoena the information so that he can give it to you.

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: Well, actually, there is no evidence in the record on whether the individual employees were audited or not.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: on that point.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: have the capacity to do it and the employer doesn't.

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: That's that's true, Justice Kennedy.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: And that's just luck, because I thought Justice O'Connor's original question was, look, we can all do this, it's so simple.

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: I'm sorry I I didn't...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: And now is there anything else...

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: is there anything else that i- di- that this restaurant owner has it in his power to do? Other than write the check.

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: A- absolutely Your Honor, and...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: What is that?

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: question.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: worker...

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: eight percent.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Right. Per month. Right.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: It is about people who satisfy all these other minimums it is not about people they can even get a tax credit for this...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: I'll save that question.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: interested in the answer to Justice O'Connor's question, in what I'd call the mind run, mainstream, basic, typical situation, and that is, what is that answer? So far I'm concluding he can...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: do nothing. Oh  tell me the answer. Yeah.

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: restaurant can do is show evidence that would tend to determine, or help determine the amount of the tips, how many wha- what kind of a restaurant you have, how upscale it is, where it's located, the kind of meals you serve in fact, the IRS has a procedure where restaurants can show all that information, a sample menu to get below the eight percent.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: To to justify in court. Right.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: I think in typical cases like this one, the restaurant will have paid more they will have assumed that it is more than eight percent.

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Now, we're only...

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: talking...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: about...

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: an area that's well above that, and in respect to the area well above that.

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: The taxpayer here had the opportunity to do that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: things that you...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: acknowledge at least that it's virtually impossible for the taxpayer to get that information? I mean, the taxpayer has the reports from the employees,

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: and that's all. And...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: how as a practical matter, is the taxpayer going to establish there's something different? I mean, we we we know in terms of generoli- in generalities, we're told that there are l- less tips paid for bills paid in cash than by a credit card.

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: That has been an assertion that is unproven in the record.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: So you don't...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: accept...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: that as proof.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: So what's the tax   so what's the employer...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: going to do?

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: It it may or may not be true.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Let me ask you one other...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: thing.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Mhm.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: system.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Right?

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: that Congress passed to address this very problem?

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: What...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: percentage of restaurants e- have used TRAC? Do the does the record tell us that?

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: No, the record doesn't tell us.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: They never did that, they didn't do it in the...

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: district court, either.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: Where would the taxpayer get the information? The only thing the taxpayer has got are the employee's returns or to the taxpayer and your assessment.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Well...

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: the taxpayer going to get the information that would allow it to do what you say in theory it could do?

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: magically, since its concession in the district court, at the appellate level and again in its briefs before this Court, the respondent has come up with all sorts of ideas that might challenge the amount of the assessment.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Even if the...

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: in this case whatever may be in the next case, there is a stipulation in this case...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Mhm.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: not contesting the method.

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: We don't know whether the taxpayer here was paying the minimum wage, because none of that was in the record, because the assessment amount was conceded.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: in this case.   Yes.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Go over that again.

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: The social security trust fund, so to speak.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Is it So to speak, yes.

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: Well, it is a matter of Government accounting.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: As as a matter of Government accounting.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: a fantasy, isn't it? I mean, it's it all goes into one pot, doesn't it?

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: A- as as a matter of Government accounting, there are funds that that are...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: So purely as an accounting...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: matter it makes a difference,

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: is that it?

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: As as a Government funding matter.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: As a Government funding matter.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: the taxpayer It d- it makes no difference, I take it.

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: From the point of view of the taxpayer, it will make a difference if they use tips to satisfy their minimum wage requirement,

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: and on this...

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: record we don't know.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: And if if they don't, it it won't make that a d- a differen-

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: It will well, the- no, that's not entirely true, because it's an optional credit.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: which they might prefer to do.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: what happens if the employer doesn't have enough gross or n- enough n- u- I guess, gross income to offs- to offset the credit? Then then he has to b- be stuck with the difference, right?

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: Well, it is a nonrefundable credit, and that means that you can either use the credit as an offset to your income tax, and what you're suggesting is they don't have any income tax.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: year.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: the qu- the question, and to be overstate the point, I'm not buying into this argument at the moment, I but I do want to hear your response.

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: Congress had the opportunity to s- say that no, the IRS does not have t- have the authority to do what has been referred to here as aggregate assessments.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: Y- You said the assessment is presumptively correct, isn't it, under the statute?

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: Not under the statute, Justice Rehnqu- Chief Justice Rehnquist, but rather under the laws that this Court has observed.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: Well, you take the position it is presumed to be correct, don't you?

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: It is u- U- United States versus Janis stands for the proposition that an assessment is valid unless it is without any foundation.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: No please go...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: ahead.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: it it has a foundation if three-quarters of it is true?

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: Pardon me?

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: It has a foundation if three-quarters of it is...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: true? Is that what having a foundation means?

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: I think is much, much lower than that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: So if...

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: this one has a foundation.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: One one penny on the dollar is accurate, that a- it has a foundation?

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Oh I I I think that would be...

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: pushing it a little far,

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Your Honor, and that's not what United States versus Janis would require.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: mean that there's some reason to believe that the full amount is accurate, not that three-quarters of it is accurate.

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: That's not what this Court has held, i- particularly in United States versus Janis, where in fact the assessment there was based on an estimate of wagering taxes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: worth of wages.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: rule assume that the taxpayer is, in fact, in a position to prove the correct figure if the assessment is wrong?

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: Not necessarily, Your Honor, and it is incumbent upon any taxpayer upon whom a tax is imposed to maintain whatever books and records...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: they can.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: comes back to the question I've asked before.

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: Well, i- the taxpayer is in a business that requires a lot of things.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: can assume that if...

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: short of the restaurant's hiring someone to bird dog every single waiter and waitress to see what, in fact, the tip was, I don't see how the employer here could collect the information.

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: The waiter...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: can also write...

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: down his tips in a little book, and there are...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: daily reports for it that the IRS provides.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: is in fact not telling the cold truth.

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: That is...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: why restaurants -- every employer should hire...

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: reliable people who they can trust to follow the rules.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: Very well, Ms. O'Connor.

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: Hm M- m- mes- may it please the Court, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: my neighbor had.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: one question that really is puzzles me in this case? I I can't follow the...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: cows and all that...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: But you did have records, written records that showed that the actual amount of the tips c- paid by credit card and so forth to the people in question here was substantially larger than you reported.

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: We do not know to what extent, if any, those credit card tip amounts, or the amount on the credit card tip slot on a credit card, was in fact received by an employee, a tip received by an employee that is wages subject to the act.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: what...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: credit cards d- they go to the restaurant, u- doesn't the restaurant turn the cash over to the employee?

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: The the res- restaurants handled it i- in a in a variety of different ways.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: We do...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: don't you know what the practice is in your own restaurants?

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: Y- we would know what what each individual restaurant would know what is done i- in basic practice in their restaurant, but how much of if any, of that credit card amount was retained by any individual employee, we...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: do not know.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: the I understand the waiter may have split the tips with the busboy and so and so on, but the total amount of tip on the credit card slip was paid to some employee, was it...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: not?

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: we don't even know whether it's a tip.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: be a tip. There...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: restaurant.

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: Would know whether that credit card amount was, in fact, a tip? No.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: circumstance where a cus-

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: on the credit card...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: As a tip? I I seem to recall seeing a...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: space on...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: restaurant charges...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: that says tip, X amount, you fill it in.

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: And that's correct, and if I went in and I didn't have any cash in my pocket and I said to the waiter, you know, I'm going to leave some extra tip on here, I need to pay for the valet when I leave, I'm going to put five bucks extra on here, can you give me the cash, yes, it's possible that there's...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: tips on there.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: The question is what's normal, and normal tip is tip, and I don't understand the chickens and the cows exactly becau- I- in may my copy of the code here it says it s- doesn't say, it says the employer, there is imposed on the employer an excise tax equal to six point two percent of wages, and it says, including tips, so I don't see how what your argument is that the tax isn't authorized.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: authorized.

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: of an individual...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: employee. It's  and they have...

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: what their assessment stands for is a is a tax on my gross tip payroll.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: that is not a tax...

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: that basic point.

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: My reply to that is, well we started out by saying it's wages, including the tips of the employee, and they did not give us a bill for wages which are the tips of the employee.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: tax.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: th- that's that's a different argument from the one that we've been wrestling with, which is whether or not you have within your auth- authority and control an a an ability to calculate the basis for challenging the assessment.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: We do not.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: began by saying, well, they they differ and, g- frankly, I I don't give much force to that argument.

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: The employer does not know how the tips, the credit card tips are distributed.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: It's not as if...

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: get the credit card receipt?

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: At the end of the...

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: the employer can set up any system the employer wants.

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: At the end of the evening the employer would cash out all the employees and would turn over credit card tips to employees who might have received credit card receipts, and those employees would then decide among themselves to whom in what amount they are going to share those tips that they have received, along with any cash...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: tips that they may have...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: has an aggregate at that point, some that appears from the credit cards to have been paid out in tips, no matter how it's...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: shared.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: he has the ability.

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: I don't really think he can make that a condition of employment.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: so forth. Those...

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: all the more all the more reason why the employer should have a strong interest in knowing how the division is being made.

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: Well, I think that there are many employees who do not want the employer to have anything to do to know to do with the tip-sharing arrangements.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Not only that, you have...

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: Power,

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: Sorry.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: has an obligation to pay FICA tax, and has an obligation to pay it on the total earnings, and it's it isn't your cow analogy didn't just pass me by, because the tax on the employer is independent of the tax on the employee.

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: So it's the employer's cow.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: belongs to the employer.

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: there is no question that the employer owes a FICA tax.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: long...

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: in fact, you stipulated, that you don't dispute the facts, the estimates, or determinations used by the IRS as a basis for its calculation of an amount of aggregate unreported tip income by all directly and indirectly tipped employees, this is yours, this on page thirty-five of the joint appendix.

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: I do not dispu- we did not dispute the amount of the IRS' aggregate assessment, because of all employees collectively, because we simply do not have the information to dispute that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: assessment in this case.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: we know that there is a disparity between what's reported and what is actually paid in tips.

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: There's plenty that the Government can do.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Congress says should be done.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: proposed, and tell me if I'm wrong about this, at that the Government go one by one after the employees, and am I correct in saying that the same method would be used by the Government if it went against an individual server, that it you are resisting the Government using against the restaurant.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: when the g-

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: entirely different, because, and as the McQuatters case that's cited in the briefs by both parties illustrates, in that circumstance, the individual waiter has an opportunity to bring any defenses he has to the Government's assessment forward, and we are denied every possible...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: defense by that scenario.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: specific, please.

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: The employee disputes the estimate.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: the tips weren't as great as you think.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: What is stiffing?

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: No no tip...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Oh.

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: Sorry.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Let's let's assume...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: the Government did...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: any of that can get you below the amount that shows up on the credit cards, because for example, say that one one employee got two thousand dollars in tips, and he could have a defense that I passed out six hundred dollars to the busboys, so maybe his liability is less, but it still seems to me the employer would have to at least pay the a- aggregate amount on the credit...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: cards. I just don't understand how you get around that.

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: there is a there's a the situation where you could have many employees.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: percentage...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: explain why a- assessments might be wrong as to individual employees, but you still doesn't reduce the gross amount in the tip column on the credit card.

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: It it does reduce the...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: gross amount...

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: that the employer would have to owe, because if any of that credit card tips that you're assuming is in effect a tip...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: was...

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: received by somebody who made less than twenty dollars a month, the employer doesn't owe any tax on...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: that. Or --

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: that's a possibility.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: But wouldn't...

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: it suffice on any rule of probability if the Government did just what Justice Stevens described? It's quite true, yes, there might have been an extraordinary turnover, if there is, the employer can come in and say so.

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: No, because you go back to thirty-one eleven and Congress did not impose a tax on i- on the aggregate earnings of all employees collectively.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Congress...

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: there is...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: that even if...

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: you had the perfect evidence, even if there was a memo beyond dispute, written by the accountant and and signed by all the employers that said, after the most thorough investigation of this restaurant I am telling you privately, and you agree, that the total tips earned that are eligible for social security are three hundred and fifty thousand, so it's signed by fifteen bishops, you know, absolute, dead right, you're saying that even if that's so, he still doesn't owe it because in the memo it doesn't say which employees.

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: I think that it needs to say which employees earned which amount, and that's what the tax was imposed upon, and if on the other hand you had all of the employees say yes, I earned this, yes, I earned this, yes, I earned this, then you would have that individual...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: determination.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: been anything held, is there, that where an employer clearly owes a tax to the Government, based because of the earnings of the employee, and there can be different contexts where that comes up, the evidence that he owes that has to name or pick out which employee? I assume if there were a case that ever said that you would have cited it, and I doubt that there is.

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: I don't think that...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: there is.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: there isn't, then that's my problem.

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: I m- I think that it's in thirty-one eleven.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: I think that this Court has...

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: already held that the tax is...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: imposed upon...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: thirty-one eleven? It's just two sentences.

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: Well, thirty-one eleven, it says wages well we- thirty-one eleven says wages I'm sorry, Your Honor, I don't have Equal to the following percentage of wages as defined in section thirty-one twenty-one A, the taxes posed on something called wages, that is defined in thirty-one twenty-one A.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: as anything but...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: thirty-one twenty-one A in...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: your brief?

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: on the next page, page fifty-six of the Government's appendix to the petition, and it says, the term wages means all remuneration for employment, and it goes on, they only have one of the the individual terms listed.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: specific...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: you're y- but you're telling us this shows your point, and you point to the language that you think it does.

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: Thirty-one eleven imposes a tax...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: on wages.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: doesn't say individual wages paid to each individual.

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: It says wages as defi- defined in...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: thirty-one twenty-one A. Thirty-one...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: we turn to thirty-one twenty-one, and where is it in there that it makes your point?

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: Be- but when you go through each one of the...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: twenty-one...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: here for, to go through something.

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: Okay, well, the foo- the twenty-one exceptions to thirty-one twenty-one A list individual things like whether an employee participates in a health insurance plan, whether an employee has a four oh one K plan, w- the exc- the extent to which an employee's wages go above or below the social...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: security wage base.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: accuracy of the figure, perhaps, which you said really is not your point.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Well, this Court has already held...

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: that that that's not the way the cu- the tax works.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: individual payment...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: two Flora cases, neither of which is cited in your brief.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: are you re-

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: versus United States, and I believe it's footnote thirty-seven in Flora versus...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: United States.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: versus United States.

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: Well, we re- referenced them in our complaint, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: paragraph fourteen of our complaint.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: Does it give a citation there?

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: Well but that was just about whether or not the tax court had jurisdiction if there if if the assessment wasn't completely paid beforehand, wasn't it?

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: Yes, but I believe that footnote thirty-seven in that belie- br- in that opinion said that the Court agreed that the excise tax, like a FICA tax, is a divisible tax.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: of...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: passed something called the TRAC law in nineteen ninety-eight to deal with this very problem of tips and the FICA tax, did it not?

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: Not technically, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: that was written between...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: there was a section in nineteen ninety-eight passed by Congress, section thirty-four fourteen of the Internal Revenue Service Restructuring and Reform Act providing that im- IRS would not threaten a taxpayer audit to coerce the taxpayer into entering a tip-reporting alternative commitment agreement, so at least Congress acknowledged...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Congress acknowledged that the a-

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: I- it acknowledged that the agreement existed,

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: but Congress...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: was assuming there were assessments going on of employers for this...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: liability?

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: that time, because there i- two documents at the very end of the joint appendix that a- are Government documents, and they indicate that the- that they were not doing the employer-only assessments at that time.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: When did the IRS start doing these aggregate assessments?

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Do you know when that started?

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: doing them about nineteen ninety-two, ninety-three and you had asked the question earlier, how many TRAC agreements are there that have been signed.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: two hundred thousand...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: provision that I asked you about was made in nineteen ninety-eight, so that was well after the beginning of aggregate assessments.

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: And long after the IRS had announced that they were not doing aggregate assessments.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: The the gravamen of your of your brief is that as a principle an assessment shouldn't be imposed on the taxpayer unless the taxpayer has the information to contradict the assessment, which certainly makes a lot of common sense.

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: Well, I think that...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Or is it...

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: just kind of a due process fairness thing?

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Well...

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: specific principle you can point to to to show the correctness of that assumption?

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: Well, I think that there's thirty years of congressional history that clearly shows that Congress did not intend for the employer to be put in this position, that Congress did not intend for the employer to be ha- to be required to police and monitor the reporting of employees, and effectively that's what the IRS's...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: interpretation does.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: you have you have no s- specific authority or precedent for the proposition that an assessment should not be imposed on a taxpayer unless the taxpayer is in a good or perhaps best position to contradict the assessment?

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: I think that we have plenty of authority for the proposition that the assessment in this case is unauthorized because it's on the collective wage-earnings...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Well but no that but that wasn't...

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: what I asked you.

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: That i- that a t- on- a tax cannot be imposed...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: upon...

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: cannot be imposed on the taxpayer unless the taxpayer has the capacity or the ability to contradict it.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Mi- the best I can tell you...

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: everybody should know, but that doesn't usually work.

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: I think that the burden of presumption and the burden of proof that they have put forth is premised upon the the u- understanding that the person with that burden has the records and is in the best position to respond, and that does not characterize this situation.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: existing here.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: situations in the tax law where the Government knows that income has been underreported? Aren't estimates made in many different contexts where the taxpayer doesn't keep reliable records, and so the Government has to f- find some way of measuring what the sha- tax should be, so it does an estimate? Isn't that...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: common?

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: one of those cases it's the the person responsible for keeping the records is the one taxed.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: and coming back and...

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: because when I asked you, are they making an estimate in that case, and you started to say no, because the the taxpayer, th- the individual servers have all these...

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: Excuses or...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: defenses?

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: They can.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: an estimate.

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: They can.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: They can...

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: that you are resisting when it's applied to the employer.

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: Because we are we don't have the same defenses that the waiter and the waitress does.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: a-

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: can the IRS are you suggesting they should go after the employees, and then when they know the amount based on the extra tax the employee will have to pay, then say, okay, employer, you paid the same amount, but practically can the IRS, does it have the facilities to go audit every waiter and busboy and...

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: There's no requirement that they audit every waiter or busboy.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: do anything about it.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: maybe it isn't cost-effective to go after the individual employees.

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: Well, I don't think it's very cost-effective to do this one,

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: when the employer is paying it out of one...

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: is owed independently, which is what I s- started to ask you about when you gave your cow example.

turns.name: TRACY J. POWER
turns.text: Because that's what Congress said...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Thank you, Ms. Power.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: Ms. O'Connor, you have three minutes remaining.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: M- Ms. O'Connor, at the very outset you said that three eleven imposed a tax on wages paid or deemed to have been paid.

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: Is the word deemed in the statute?

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: I believe so.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: Well, I'll f- I'll I'll find it, if you the...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Yes.  I- page on page...

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: fifty-eight A thirty-one twenty is where thirty-one twenty-one Q appears, and remember that is a section that provides definitions.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: Well, except I think the deeming refers to the time of payment, not to the fact of payment, but I'll I'll look at that.

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: I think you're right about that, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: It's at page fifty-eight A.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: been m- making these assessments, please,

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Ms. O'Connor?

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: that it did start around nineteen ninety-two and nineteen ninety-three, and the information that the IRS is gathering on the forms eighty twenty-seven demonstrated the extent of the problem that Congress suspected did exist about substantial underreporting of tip income.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: In view of all the questions about the proper computation, why has the Government resisted notice and comment rulemaking to come up with a fair formula, because the the contention is that the Government's formula exaggerates the income, exaggerates the tip income.

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: Th- that is certainly the contention, and there have been, however, no facts to show whether that's that's true or not, because in each of the cases, not only the Ninth Circuit below, but in each of the other three cases that we cite in our brief decided by the Seventh, Eleventh, and the Federal Circuits, no evidence was ever submitted to show that the tips were overstated...

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: in any...

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: why not em- employ that fair procedure of going through the notice and comment, and then I think that was what Judge Kozinski said would do.

turns.name: EILEEN J. O'CONNOR
turns.text: Yes, he did.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: entirely excludes section...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: Judge Kozinski did say that, so the case is submitted.
